quick_facts.label: Role focus
quick_facts.value: In one documented municipal-health implementation, a Community Development Officer supervises reproductive and child-health work, monitors and promotes family-planning activity, leads information/education/communication activity, trains health workers and community volunteers, assists with population-census work, and supervises urban and national health-mission activities in a ward.

quick_facts.label: Community Science route
quick_facts.value: B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science is a four-year professional programme at the cited agricultural university. Its curriculum includes food and nutrition, human development and family studies, resource management, extension education and communication management; the university identifies planning, implementation and evaluation of development programmes including ICDS as a professional competency.

quick_facts.label: MSW route
quick_facts.value: MSW programmes can provide supervised community-facing practice: the cited curriculum includes community organization, research methods and statistics, urban and rural community development, fieldwork, reporting and a block placement/internship.

quick_facts.label: Employer-dependent eligibility
quick_facts.value: There is no single India-wide entry rule evidenced by the sources. A Himachal Pradesh project engagement preferred a postgraduate diploma in social work plus community-mobilisation experience, or substantial graduate-level experience; an Uttarakhand project advertisement specified MSW/social-science postgraduate qualifications and more than five years’ experience for a Social Community Development Officer.

quick_facts.label: Compensation context
quick_facts.value: Pay is employer, location, contract and seniority dependent. As a dated example rather than a market benchmark, a 2026 Himachal Pradesh project notice listed maximum emoluments of ₹45,599 for Community Development Officer and Assistant Community Development Officer positions.

sections.heading: What a Community Development Officer does
paragraphs: Community Development Officer is a cross-sector title rather than a single nationally standardised job description. In a municipal public-health setting, the role is ward-based and combines programme supervision with community mobilisation: reproductive and child-health programme oversight, family-planning monitoring and promotion, information/education/communication (IEC) work, training for health-care workers and community volunteers, support for census activity, and supervision of health-mission activities.

The common thread is converting welfare or development programme objectives into local delivery. That usually means identifying community needs, coordinating frontline workers and partner organisations, conducting outreach and training, keeping programme records, and monitoring implementation. The precise subject area—such as nutrition, child and family welfare, livelihoods, public health, women’s development or rural/urban development—depends on the employing department or project.
bullets: Plan and coordinate local programme activities and community outreach.
Mobilise residents, volunteers, self-help groups or other local stakeholders where the programme requires it.
Deliver or coordinate IEC and capacity-building activities.
Monitor field implementation, maintain records and prepare reports for programme managers.
Work with public-service staff, NGOs and community institutions; responsibilities may include health, nutrition, family and child welfare, or development-project activities.

sections.heading: Entry routes from Community Science and Social Work
paragraphs: B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science is a relevant foundation where the intended work involves family development, nutrition, extension, communication or community programmes. ANGRAU describes the degree as a four-year professional course covering food and nutrition, human development and family studies, resource management, extension education and communication management; it also identifies planning, implementation and evaluation of programmes such as ICDS among its competencies. Assam Agricultural University similarly describes a four-year programme with practical elements including Rural Home Science Work Experience, experiential learning and industrial attachment.

MSW is a strong route where the role calls for social-work methods, community organisation, project work and supervised field practice. The cited MSW curriculum includes community organisation, social-work research and statistics, urban and rural community development, compulsory fieldwork, reports and a one-month block placement/internship.

Applicants should treat each vacancy notice as controlling. Project employers may require prior community-mobilisation experience or an MSW/social-science postgraduate qualification, whereas some Community Science graduates may enter adjacent coordinator, extension or community-worker roles and build relevant field experience before applying for officer posts.
bullets: From B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science: prioritise extension/community communication, food and nutrition, human development and family studies, and documented fieldwork or rural-work experience.
From MSW: prioritise community organisation/development specialisation, field practicum, research/report-writing and placements in government, NGO or community settings.
Build evidence of field delivery: outreach plans, training facilitation, beneficiary/community engagement, monitoring records and reports.
Check the specific recruiting body’s required degree, experience, local-language and contract conditions before applying.

sections.heading: Skills to develop
paragraphs: The role calls for a blend of community-facing practice and programme-management discipline. The municipal role description supports the importance of programme supervision, IEC, training and coordination with health workers and volunteers. Community Science curricula support nutrition, family-development and extension/communication knowledge, while the MSW curriculum supports community organisation, fieldwork, reporting, research and monitoring.
For entry-level candidates, a portfolio of supervised fieldwork, outreach materials, training plans, community assessments and concise field reports can make these capabilities visible to employers.
bullets: Community mobilisation, facilitation and respectful communication with diverse groups.
Programme planning, implementation monitoring and basic evaluation.
IEC material development and delivery of awareness sessions.
Training and support for frontline workers, volunteers or community groups.
Field documentation, report writing, record maintenance and basic data interpretation.
Knowledge relevant to the post’s sector, such as nutrition, family and child welfare, public health, livelihoods, rural development or urban community development.

sections.heading: Where the role may be based
paragraphs: Workplaces can include municipal or state public-service programmes, project implementation units, health and welfare departments, rural-development institutions, NGOs and other community-serving organisations. In the Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation example, the post is embedded in a ward health department and works alongside a Medical Officer of Health. Agricultural-university career information also identifies social-welfare, health-mission, government and non-government organisations as relevant employment settings for Community Science graduates.

This is predominantly field-linked work. Time may be divided between community visits, meetings and training sessions on one hand, and office-based planning, coordination, records and reporting on the other.
bullets: Municipal and urban public-health or family-welfare programmes.
Social-welfare and women-and-child-development programmes.
Rural or urban development projects and project implementation units.
NGOs, community-based organisations and CSR-supported development programmes.
Community-facing roles that combine local fieldwork with programme reporting.

sections.heading: Progression and adjacent roles
paragraphs: Progression is employer-specific rather than uniform across India. The existence of titles such as Assistant Community Development Officer, Community Development Officer, Social Community Development Officer and Senior Community Development Officer in public-project and metropolitan-development recruitment materials indicates that responsibilities and seniority can be differentiated by organisation.

A practical progression path is to deepen field-delivery experience first, then take on larger geographic areas, more complex programme portfolios, staff or volunteer supervision, monitoring responsibilities, and project coordination. Community Science and MSW curricula both support further specialisation in areas relevant to this progression, including extension/communication management and urban/rural community development.
bullets: Possible adjacent entry roles: community worker, extension officer, community-development coordinator or project-field staff.
Potential advancement depends on the employer and may involve senior community-development, programme-coordination, monitoring or project-management responsibilities.
Further study or specialisation can be useful where a vacancy requires a postgraduate social-work qualification or advanced sector expertise.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: Compensation should be evaluated vacancy by vacancy because Community Development Officer posts may be regular public-sector posts, fixed-term project engagements or employer-specific NGO/CSR roles. In a 2026 Himachal Pradesh project expression of interest, the listed maximum emoluments for Community Development Officer and Assistant Community Development Officer were ₹45,599; the same notice described the engagement as requirement-based and not a claim to regular employment. This is a specific, dated project example, not a national salary standard.

When comparing offers, verify whether the stated amount is basic pay, consolidated monthly remuneration or a maximum project emolument; also check tenure, benefits, travel or field allowances, location and experience requirements.
bullets: Do not treat a single advertised figure as a national benchmark.
Fixed-term project remuneration and permanent public-service pay structures can differ materially.
Higher experience requirements, specialist subject knowledge and responsibility for larger programmes may affect the advertised level.
